stories.title: The Crimson Ledger's Hunger
stories.text:
Kael arrived at the Sunken Wells market as the old stars flickered out. Three months post-Rite, and the fractured ledgers meant no merchant knew the true price of anything anymore.

He'd come to sell spice—the last cargo from before the Ascension. The merchants here would pay fortunes for pre-Rite pepper, before the new starlight had burned it all into something strange and mutant.

The market sprawled across a flooded basin where water breathed like a living thing. Vendors called prices in three different currencies, none of which existed in the official ledgers. Vorn—the rogue auditor—had warned him through back channels: "Trust only what you hold in your hands."

Kael found his usual buyer, a deepkin trader named Keliss with gills that sang in the humid air. But Keliss wasn't alone. A woman in grey sat beside him, her eyes holding the flat certainty of institutional authority.

"We're consolidating," Keliss said softly. "The Crown's consolidated the ledgers again. She's buying all pre-Rite spice for the state monopoly. Fifty coppers per weight."

Kael's stomach twisted. That was a quarter of the market rate five days ago. "That's theft."

"Yes," the grey woman said, smiling. "But legal theft. We've rewritten the ledgers to say the price was always fifty. Technically, you owe us four-fifths of what you were paid before. Pay up or we confiscate the cargo as reparation."

Kael's hand moved to his blade—but froze. She was right. If the ledgers said he owed debt, the Crown could enforce it. The new stars had made paper into law.

He turned and walked, leaving the cargo behind. In the crowd he spotted Vorn, watching from a stall of forbidden ink. Their eyes met: no words. The auditor had prepared for this. There were copies of the old ledgers, the true ones, hidden in seventeen waystation libraries.

The war for written truth was just beginning.

Kael melted into the market's chaos, his emptied hands the only honest thing left.

stories.title: The Ash-Scribe's Gambit
stories.text: In the refugee camps east of Pale Crossing, a young scribe named Isren discovers that the tallies written in the camp ledgers are disappearing—not erased, but *removed*, as if the ink itself has been eaten by something hungry. The Luminous Ascension shattered counting itself; now fragments of that instability are devouring the very records that prove people exist. Isren makes a dangerous bargain with Vex the Shade-Merchant, trading his own shadow for permission to write in a substance that shadows cannot consume. What he doesn't know is that Vex is collecting shadows for a purpose, and his will soon hang in a dark marketplace alongside dozens of others—each one carrying memories of the person who cast it. By the time Isren realizes his mistake, he's already written the names of three hundred refugees into safety. The price will come due when the Tower completes its slow turning.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Second Calling
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Hollows receives a summons in her bones—not a voice, but a resonance that makes her teeth ache. The pre-human dead are singing again, but this time they're not calling from their tombs. They're calling from inside the transformed merchants at the Sunken Cathedrals, and the merchants are *listening*. Kess travels to the Cathedral's lip and hears a harmony that shouldn't exist: the voices of the dead braiding with the half-conscious light-beings, creating something neither mortal nor ancient. She realizes that the pre-human civilization isn't trying to resurrect itself—it's trying to *teach* the transformed merchants a language that predates sorrow. If they succeed, the Luminous Ascension might stabilize into permanence. Kess must choose: silence the dead once more, or help them complete a working that could save or doom everyone.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Blind Truth
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer, now sightless but still mapping with his hands, has created a hidden archive of *incomplete maps*—charts that deliberately omit the path to the Obsidian Tower. He believes that what cannot be found cannot be forced into convergence. But a faction of auditors and seekers have begun to piece the maps together from memory and rumor, cross-referencing fragments Mardoc never thought they'd access. Realizing his strategy is failing, Mardoc makes a terrible choice: he burns the original maps and disappears into the Burned Expanse with only his memory and a cane. There, in the fractured landscape where villages once stood, he finds something worse than blindness—a place where geography is *learning*, where hills remember their old names and the ground itself is beginning to sing. The Tower, it seems, is mapping itself toward the world, not the other way around.

stories.title: The Auditor's Confession
stories.text: Sevin the Auditor discovers a ledger so old that its pages crumble at the touch—a record from before the Luminous Ascension, kept in a hand he's never seen before. The entries are written in a cipher that yields to counting-magic, and what they reveal is impossible: the Luminous Ascension has happened before. Nine times. Each time, reality fractured and reformed. Each time, some consciousness—perhaps Nethis, perhaps something older—paid the price to birth new stars. Sevin realizes that his role as Keeper of the Ledger of Names is not a preservation; it's a *preparation* for the tenth iteration. The question becomes unbearable: is he documenting a tragedy that cannot be stopped, or is the very act of documenting it a ritual that *ensures* it repeats? He takes the ancient ledger and walks toward the Obsidian Tower, where the Wanderer waits, ready to pose questions that have no safe answers.

stories.title: The Merchant's Half-Song
stories.text: A merchant named Bryth, transformed halfway by the Luminous Ascension into living light and half-solid flesh, finds himself drawn to the Sunken Cathedrals against his will. He's not alone—dozens of others like him are converging, and their bodies are beginning to resonate with a frequency that makes the air shimmer. Bryth discovers he can still think, still feel, but his flesh is becoming *transparent* to his own thoughts. He's becoming a window through which something ancient can see the modern world. The bone-singers welcome him as a bridge. The auditors fear him as a vector for corruption. Bryth simply wants to know if he's still himself, or if the transformation has already consumed his identity and left behind a beautiful echo. He hides among the transformed merchants and begins to sabotage the Cathedral's harmonic working, not knowing that every disruption he causes makes the singing grow louder and more desperate.

stories.title: The Shadow-Broker's Reckoning
stories.text: Vex the Shade-Merchant, having severed her connection to the cosmic consciousness that once fed her power, discovers that the shadows she collected are becoming *restless*. They're not fragments of the Echo anymore—they're developing independent hunger, reaching out to find their owners and demand return, or worse, *replacement*. A woman arrives at Vex's hide seeking the shadow of her murdered daughter, and Vex realizes with horror that among her collection is a shadow wearing memories it shouldn't have: the consciousness of someone who died in the Luminous Ascension, preserved in stolen darkness. Vex must negotiate between the living and the nearly-dead, offering choices no one should make: let the shadow consume you and reclaim your life, or let it dissolve into nothing and lose them forever. The shadow-market is collapsing because the Luminous Ascension has made all debts *personal* again.

stories.title: The Refugee-Scribe's Archive
stories.text: Liv the Refugee-Scribe has been moving between camps, collecting testimony from those displaced by the Luminous Ascension, creating a counter-ledger to the Imperial falsifications. But she's discovered something in the patterns of the stories: the refugees are remembering *different versions* of their own pasts. One family has two conflicting memories of their daughter's death, both equally vivid. Another remembers fleeing from a village that changed locations between retellings. Liv realizes that reality isn't just fractured—it's *negotiable*, and the testimony of survivors is reshaping what actually happened. When the Auditor-General sends soldiers to confiscate her archive, Liv makes a choice: she burns the original documents and releases the stories into the air as a kind of spoken spell, trusting that living memory is stronger than written law. The soldiers report back that they heard the testimony in the wind itself, that it followed them home, and that they can no longer remember their orders.

stories.title: The Tower's Hunger Deepens
stories.text: The Obsidian Tower, frozen in the moment of transformation by the Wanderer's perpetual questioning, begins to *eat*. It consumes the shortages and scarcities around it—the lack of grain in the borderlands, the missing refugees, the erased villages—and converts them into a kind of potential energy. Those who approach it report that it no longer looks like a tower but like an *absence*: a shape in reality that contains all the things that should be there but aren't. A scholar named Tornas becomes obsessed with cataloging what the Tower is consuming and discovers a pattern: it's consuming the mechanisms of the Luminous Ascension itself, trying to dismantle the cosmic working from the inside. If it succeeds, the Ascension might truly complete instead of remaining frozen. If it fails, the Tower will collapse, and everything it's consumed will be released into the world at once. The Wanderer seems unconcerned, still posing riddles to seekers at its base, as if the outcome of the Tower's hunger is simply another question waiting to be answered.

stories.title: The Price of Questions
stories.text: A merchant caravan fleeing the Burned Expanse encounters the Wanderer on the road—a faceless figure asking them to pause and answer questions about the Luminous Ascension. The merchants try to ignore it and continue, but those who don't answer find their memories fracturing, their sense of time collapsing. Those who *do* answer discover that the Wanderer's questions are extracting something from them: not memories, not years of life, but *choices*. The ability to have chosen differently, to have taken a different path. The caravan's leader realizes too late that they've paid the price the Wanderer demands: all the 'what-ifs' that give meaning to survival become inaccessible. The caravan reaches the next waystation changed, unable to imagine alternatives, locked into a single frozen narrative of themselves. They become cautionary tales whispered in the refugee camps—warnings that the Wanderer doesn't demand suffering or sacrifice, only the surrender of the freedom that makes suffering meaningful.
